item.title: AWS Weekly Roundup: Amazon Aurora 10th anniversary, Amazon EC2 R8 instances, Amazon Bedrock and more (August 25, 2025)
item.author: Betty Zheng (郑予彬)
item.content:encoded: As I was preparing for this week’s roundup, I couldn’t help but reflect on how database technology has evolved over the past decade. It’s fascinating to see how architectural decisions made years ago continue to shape the way we build modern applications. This week brings a special milestone that perfectly captures this evolution in cloud database innovation as Amazon Aurora celebrated 10 years of database innovation.Amazon Web Services (AWS) Vice President Swami Sivasubramanian reflected on LinkedIn about his journey with Amazon Aurora, calling it “one of the most interesting products” he’s worked on. When Aurora launched in 2015, it shifted the database landscape by separating compute and storage. Now trusted by hundreds of thousands of customers across industries, Aurora has grown from a MySQL-compatible database to a comprehensive platform featuring innovations such as Aurora DSQL, serverless capabilities, I/O-Optimized pricing, zero-ETL integrations, and generative AI support. Last week’s celebration on August 21 highlighted this decade-long transformation that continues to simplify database scaling for customers.In addition to the inspiring celebrations, here are some AWS launches that caught my attention:AWS Billing and Cost Management introduces customizable Dashboards — This new feature consolidates cost data into visual dashboards with multiple widget types and visualization options, combining information from Cost Explorer, Savings Plans, and Reserved Instance reports to help organizations track spending patterns and share standardized cost reporting across accounts.Amazon Bedrock simplifies access to OpenAI open weight models — AWS has streamlined access to OpenAI’s open weight models (gpt-oss-120b and gpt-oss-20b), making them automatically available to all users without manual activation while maintaining administrator control through IAM policies and service control policies.AWS launching Amazon EC2 R8i and R8i-flex memory-optimized instances — Powered by custom Intel Xeon 6 processors, these new instances deliver up to 20 percent better performance and 2.5 times higher memory throughput than R7i instances, making them ideal for memory-intensive workloads like databases and big data analytics, with R8i-flex offering additional cost savings for applications that don’t fully utilize compute resources.Amazon S3 introduces batch data verification feature — A new capability in S3 Batch Operations that offers efficient verification of billions of objects using multiple checksum algorithms without downloading or restoring data, generating detailed integrity reports for compliance and audit purposes regardless of storage class or object size.Here are some additional projects and blog posts that you might find interesting:Building Strands Agents with a few lines of code — A new blog demonstrates how to build multi-agent AI systems with a few lines of code, enabling specialized agents to collaborate seamlessly, handle complex workflows, and share information through standardized protocols for creating distributed AI systems beyond individual agent capabilities.AWS Security Incident Response introduces ITSM integrations — New integrations with Jira and ServiceNow provide bidirectional synchronization of security incidents, comments, and attachments, streamlining response while maintaining existing processes, with open source code available on GitHub for customization and extension to additional IT service management (ITSM) platforms.Finding root-causes using a network digital twin graph and agentic AI — A detailed blog post shows how AWS collaborated with NTT DOCOMO to build a network digital twin using graph databases and autonomous AI agents, helping telecom operators to move beyond correlation to identify true root causes of complex network issues, predict future problems, and improve overall service reliability. Check your calendars and sign up for these upcoming AWS events:AWS Summits — Join free online and in-person events that bring the cloud computing community together to connect, collaborate, and learn about AWS. Register in your nearest city: Toronto (September 4), Los Angeles (September 17), and Bogotá (October 9).AWS re:Invent 2025 — This flagship annual conference is coming to Las Vegas from December 1–5. The event catalog is now available. Mark your calendars for this not to be missed gathering of the AWS community.That’s all for this week. Check back next Monday for another Weekly Roundup!

item.title: What Is a Foreign Key Constraint in SQL?
item.author: Dbschema Pro
item.content:encoded: A is a validation rule in the database that links one table to another by matching values.It helps keep your data linked correctly and stops you from adding values that don’t exist.Imagine you have two tables:One called Orders, where you track purchases - like order number, date, and total.Another called Customers, where each customer has a unique ID, along with their name and contact details.To know which customer placed each order, you add a customer_id column to the Orders table.But just adding the column isn’t enough. You need to it to the Customers table.You do this by creating a — a link between Orders.customer_id and Customers.customer_id (the ).The ensures that the values in Orders.customer_id in Customers.customer_id.The database won’t allow you to insert an order with a customer ID that doesn’t exist in the Customers table.If you try, it will return an error.This helps keep your data clean and consistent.Customer ID = 52 (Tiffany Ritter) placed Order #33 on 10.01.2000.Thanks to the foreign key, we can link this order back to the correct customer to see what she bought.Now, imagine we try to insert an order with a customer_id of into the Orders table, but there's no customer with ID 1000 in the Customers table.Because the foreign key is in place, the database checks if that customer exists first.Since it doesn’t, the database blocks the insert and shows an error like the one below.This is called — it means the database won’t let you create links to data that doesn’t exist.Syntax: Foreign Key in SQLLet’s consider that we have two tables: Customers and Orders.CREATE TABLE Orders ( OrderID INT PRIMARY KEY, CustomerID INT,CREATE TABLE Customers ( CustomerID INT PRIMARY KEY, Email VARCHAR(100));Now, let’s add a foreign key between these tables:ALTER TABLE OrdersADD CONSTRAINT fk_orders_customersFOREIGN KEY (CustomerID) REFERENCES Customers(CustomerID);In this case, Orders.CustomerIDis the foreign key.It must match a value from Customers.CustomerID - the primary key.Creating Foreign Keys Visually in DbSchemaIn DbSchema, you don’t need to write SQL by hand. You can drag a line from one table to another and define the relationship visually.Open your project or reverse-engineer the database.Drag the referring column of the primary key column from the (e.g. Orders.CustomerID) to the (e.g. Customers.CustomerID).DbSchema will create a foreign key for you.It’s simple, visual, and you immediately see how tables connect.What If the Foreign Key Doesn’t Exist?In some databases (especially NoSQL or legacy systems), foreign keys might not exist.DbSchema lets you create virtual foreign keys — relationships that exist only in the diagram, not in the actual database.Understanding data relationshipsBuilding queries visually without needing actual constraintsViewing data from related tables easilyForeign keys do more than just link tables; they .They make sure that every reference in your database points to something real. No missing customers, no orphaned orders, no broken relationships.When you use foreign keys:Your data becomes more .Your SQL queries become easier to write and understand.Your database design is clearer for you and your team.And with free tools like DbSchema, you don’t need to write complex SQL to define them — you can create and even simulate foreign keys visually, making design and documentation faster than ever.If you want to learn more about foreign keys, check out these helpful resources:These explain the concept with more exThese explain the concept with more examples, syntax differences, and best practices.

item.title: Azure: Serverless website deployment 100% automated in multiple environments (Test, QA and…
item.author: Olusegun Omotunde
item.content:encoded: Azure: Serverless website deployment 100% automated in multiple environments (Test, QA and Production) using Azure DevOps Repos and Pipelines (CI/CD)In this project, I served as a Cloud DevOps Engineer, responsible for implementing a fully automated, serverless static website deployment pipeline using Azure DevOps. The goal was to establish a streamlined CI/CD process that supports three deployment environments — Testing, QA, and Production — without relying on traditional virtual machine infrastructure.🛠️ 2. Project Description and ObjectiveThe project centered on deploying a static website hosted on an Azure Storage Account (blob type) configured for static website hosting. The approach was entirely serverless, meaning no virtual machines were used reducing cost, increasing scalability, and simplifying maintenance.Setting up a new Azure DevOps Organization and Project.Creating and pushing website source files to Azure Repos (Git).Defining CI/CD pipelines using Azure Pipelines:Continuous Integration (CI): Automatically triggered on code commits to build and validate changes.Continuous Delivery (CD): Automatically deploys validated code to the static site in Testing, QA, and Production environments.Each pipeline stage was configured to deploy to a separate Azure Storage Account (tcbaf1tst, tcbaf1qa, tcbaf1prd) using service connections and YAML-defined pipeline templates.This serverless model ensures that the website is centrally managed, version-controlled, and deployed consistently across environments, enabling rapid development and safer production releases.🧱 3. Architecture Diagram🧭 4. Step-by-Step Implementation WalkthroughCreating a storage account for each environment:Create three Azure storage accounts (testing, qa, production environment) • Testing: tcbos1tst • QA: tcbos1qaunder the resource group ‘azurecloudbootcamp’ in the ‘East US’ region. All the Advanced, Networking, Data protection, and Encryption with default values and no tag settings.Enabling Static Website | Setting up index.html as home pageUnder Data management settings, enable static website and set the default index document name as index.html for the three storage accounts.After creation, each storage account will expose its static website via the respective :Click on ‘ My Azure DevOps Organization’Click on ‘create new organization’Type a name for your ‘Azure Devops Organization’ from “We’ll host your projects in”, I used US East Region. Insert your project name in our case, website01, from ‘Visibility’ select ‘private’, and click on ‘Create Project’.Now we can request a “Free parallelism grant for the DevOps Organization”Insert the name of your ‘Azure DevOps organization’ and ‘private’ for projectsInside this ‘website01’ project, Azure will provide the Boards for planning the development tasks, Repos to store the application source code like static website details in our case, Pipelines to configure continuous integration and continuous delivery settings, Test Plans to set up test cases to perform testing on current development, Artifacts to hold the build files and dependencies ready for deployment.Generating a Password for Azure repository (Generate Git Credentials):We need to generate password for Azure repository to upload the website source codes & files sitting in azure devops. Go to repos on the azure Devops page and lick on generate credentials. Save the password which will be used later to authenticate from the local developer client workstation.Clone into the Git hub repository to get the application source code & files.git clone https://github.com/thecloudbootcamp/azuredevops.gitGit pushing the code to Azure repos:Push the source code to Azure repos under ‘azuredevops’ folder with Git credentials to authenticate to Azure Repos using the command line password. The command to push an existing repository from command line is provided on the Azure DevOps Repo page.Check these files are successfully uploaded using git push command to Azure Repos.cd azuredevopsgit remote add origin https://tcbolusegun2025@dev.azure.com/tcbolusegun2025/website01/_git/website01If you get the error above, you just need to run and run the command again. You will authenticate with the auto-generated password from the repos pagecd azuredevopsgit remote remove origingit remote add origin https://tcbolusegun2025@dev.azure.com/tcbolusegun2025/website01/_git/website01git push -u origin --allWe can now see the website files on the azure DevOps.Create Continuous Integration Pipeline | CI:Create a CI pipeline so that the changes made in the source code from my local client developer machine can be pushed automatically to azure repos. This should initiate a build process so that the deployment process can be started by deploying the application into the newly created storage account.We are going to use the classic editor not the new YAML‑based pipelines experience in Azure DevOps (sometimes called the “modern” UI).By default, the classic editor will be disabled. Go to organization and under pipeline settings, enable the classic editor by:Disable creation of classic build pipelinesDisable creation of classic release pipelinesGo to pipelines and create a CI pipeline using a classic editor with the Empty job template.select Azure repos git as source for codeAdd archival files task from agent job 1 taskChang the display name of this task to ‘Zip files’select the file folder in the website01 as the root folder to archive.We are going to add a new task called publish build artifact task next and change the display name to Artifacts Publish and artifact name as ‘drop’ to create in the publish location.Save the continuous integration pipeline website01-CI after checking and confirming all the configuration settings.Save the pipeline only not save and queueThe last step is to enable the continuous integration under the Triggers tab in the CI pipeline. The Continous Integration will constantly check for changes in source code and if changes are detected, it will start new release of our code.Creating Continuous Deployment PipelineThe continous deployment pipeline is responsible for deploying the websites to the storage accounts just createdNaivgate to releases under pipeline and create a new release for the pipeline.Select an Empty job template to create the release pipeline.Add an artifact and select the source build pipeline the continuous integration pipeline that publishes the build artifacts.Click the Artifacts trigger Icon to enable continuous deployment trigger, so that whenever there is a change in source code (website interface, color or design changes) then this release pipeline will recognize the code change and initiate the deployment of all the changes and trigger a new release on all the configured staging environments like Testing, QA, and Production.We are going to add and configure the testing stage. The testing stage has all steps that pipeline needs to execute to deploy the website on the storage account in the testing environment.Click on stage1 and rename to testingClick on the testing stage and we are going to add two tasks in this testing stage environment. Click on agent job and add ‘extract files task’ and ‘azure cli task’Extract files will extract the zip files created from continous integration. Remember we created a zip file task in CI pipeline to push changes in source code from local client developer machine and put in azure directory.Adding task: Extract filesChnange the Display name: Extract files from websiteArchive file patterns: */$(Build.BuildId).zipDestination folder: $(Build.DefaultWorkingDirectory)/$(Build.BuildId)We are going to add the Azure CLI task next:Display name: Upload files to Azure BlobAuthorizing: Azure Resource Manager connection…Script location: Inline scriptaz storage blob upload-batch --account-name tcbos1tst --destination "\$web" --source ./files --overwriteThe inline script is the azure client command that we need to give so azure cli can upload the files inside the files folder from the repository to the storage account we have created under the test environment where the websites files will be uploadedRemember to replace the account name with the actual name of your storage account in the testing environment. My storage account name is tcbos1tst hence: ‘az storage blob upload-batch — account-name tcbos1tst — destination “\$web” — source ./files — overwrite’Working Directory: $(Build.DefaultWorkingDirectory)/$(Build.BuildId)Rename pipeline to website01-CD and saveCloning Stage (QA): Clone the existing testing stage to the QA stage. By cloning we are saving timeChange the name from copy to QARemember to rename the storage account in the inline script to QA storage account.Cloning Stage (Production): Clone the existing QA to the Production stage.Click on TasksClick on Upload files to Azure BlobRename storage account in the inline script to Production storage accountand saveFrom the Production stage, Enable Pre-deployment conditions to get approval before deploying to production.Pre-deployment approvals: put your Azure access emailNow anytime the website source code changes, the pipelines tasks will be executed automatically. The release pipeline will detect changes and deploy changes in test enviornment, the QA environment and when someone approves the changes in production environment, it will be deployed to productionSelect pipeline website01-CI and run the pipelineThe pipeline is currently running the tasksIt shows that all the configured tasks that are executed.When we check the release pipeline ‘website01-CD’, we can see that it is initiating the release for the Testing stageDuring this Azure DevOps release pipeline, I ran into an error when trying to upload static website files to Azure Blob Storage using this command:After checking the logs and examining the file paths, I realized the issue stemmed from a mismatch between where the files were being extracted and where the upload task expected them to be.My Extract files from website task extracted contents to the root of the working directory: $(Build.DefaultWorkingDirectory)/$(Build.BuildId) but the task was trying to upload from a subdirectory called ./files, which did not exist after extraction.az storage blob upload-batch — account-name tcbos1tst — destination “\$web” — source ./files — overwrite failed because ./files was not foundI changed the Archive file patterns in the extract files from website tasks to correctly find the .zip file: **/*.zip.Changed */$(Build.BuildId).zip to **/*.zip.I updated the destination folder in the extract task to be a consistent and known location in testing, qa and production stage:$(System.DefaultWorkingDirectory)/unzippedI updated the Working directory in the upload files to Azure blob task to match the path from the extract task in testing, qa and production stages$(System.DefaultWorkingDirectory)/unzippedI updated source directory in the Azure CLI command of upload files to Azure blob task to upload from the root of the folder in the testing, qa and production stages:testing: az storage blob upload-batch — account-name tcbos1tst — destination “\$web” — source . — overwriteqa: az storage blob upload-batch — account-name tcbos1qa — destination “\$web” — source . — overwriteproduction: az storage blob upload-batch — account-name tcbos1prd — destination “\$web” — source . — overwriteaz storage blob upload-batch --account-name tcbos1tst --destination "\$web" --source . --overwriteAfter updating, refresh and redeployApprove pre-deployment and the production stage release is completed.All release pipeline stages completed successfully.Deployment is successfully. we can check the storage account, under the data storage for the testing, qa and Production environment and we can see all the files were pushed here by the release pipeline tasks.we will validate url byccessing the static website from the Testing storage account and do the same for QA and production as well.We can access the static website under the data management tab under storage accounts of the test, qa and production. Open the url provided by the primary end pointWe can now acccess the static website from the testing, qa and Production storage account.we are going to make changes in the index.html file by editing the existing text ‘Congratulations!’ to ‘Good job!’ , save the changes and push the changes to Azure Repo ‘website01’ using the following git commands:vi files/index.html from local developer machine and edit the existing text 'Congratulations!' to 'Good job!'git add files/index.htmlgit config - global user.email "patrickevra@gmail.com" git commit -m "index.html change"We can check Azure Repos for ‘website01’ repo. The commit shows the index.html file has changed. These changes will trigger the build pipeline for continuous integration which will eventually trigger the release pipeline as the build artifacts have changed.Access the static website from the Production storage account.The static website has been successfully deployed.By the end of the project, the following objectives were successfully achieved:A functional Azure DevOps organization and project were created and configured.Website files were securely version-controlled in Azure Repos.Fully automated CI/CD pipelines were deployed to handle build and release processes.The static website was hosted serverlessly on three environment-specific Azure Storage Accounts (Testing, QA, and Production).Developers and stakeholders can now access the site through the respective primary endpoints, with changes automatically deployed upon approved commits.No virtual machines were used, highlighting the effectiveness of serverless architecture for hosting and deploying static content.6. Azure Services and Technologies (Overview & Explanation)Azure IoT Hub is a that acts as a between IoT applications and their connected devices. It provides secure and reliable bi-directional communication, supporting millions of devices. This is essential for remote monitoring, telemetry, and command control.Azure IoT Central is a ready-to-use IoT application platform that simplifies the development, deployment, and management of large-scale IoT solutions. It eliminates the need for custom back-end development, reducing time to market and complexity.Azure Sphere is a secure, high-level application platform with built-in communication and security features designed specifically for Internet-connected devices. It ensures hardware-to-cloud security by integrating microcontrollers, an OS, and a cloud security service.Azure Synapse is an that combines enterprise data warehousing and . It enables querying of both relational and non-relational data using serverless or provisioned resources and provides deep insights across data lakes and databasesAzure HDInsight is a cloud-based, fully managed open-source analytics service. It supports multiple big data frameworks such as Hadoop, Spark, Hive, Kafka, Storm, and R. It’s ideal for processing massive amounts of data using familiar open-source technologies.Azure Databricks is an Apache Spark-based analytics platform that offers a collaborative workspace for data engineers, scientists, and machine learning developers. It supports large-scale data processing, real-time analytics, and model training in a unified environment.Azure Cognitive Services are a suite of accessible via APIs to add capabilities such as vision, speech, language, decision-making, and search to applications. These services simplify the integration of AI into both web and enterprise apps.Azure Machine Learning is a cloud-based machine learning platform used to build, train, and deploy predictive models. It streamlines the entire ML lifecycle, from data preparation to deployment, using a drag-and-drop ML designer or full-code notebooks.Azure Bot Service allows developers to build and deploy intelligent, conversational bots using text, cards, or speech. It supports customer service automation and integrates with channels like Microsoft Teams, Slack, and web apps.Azure Logic Apps is a workflow automation platform for integrating apps, data, services, and systems using a visual designer and triggers. It enables serverless orchestration of complex business processes across multiple services and APIs.Azure DevOps provides an end-to-end set of to plan, build, test, and deploy applications. It includes services such as , , , , and , supporting agile practices and CI/CD.Azure DevTest Labs enables teams to quickly create preconfigured dev/test environments using templates. It helps control cost, automate provisioning, and streamline testing without delays from central IT.GitHub and GitHub ActionsGitHub is a built on Git, allowing collaborative development through repositories, commits, branches, and pull requests. is an integrated used to automate workflows directly within the GitHub ecosystem.Azure Cost Management + Pricing Tools: Provides dashboards and analytics to track, forecast, and optimize cloud spending.: Allows users to before deployment.: Compares on-prem vs. Azure costs for .Azure Service Level Agreements (SLA) and Lifecycle: Defines availability and performance guarantees for each Azure service.Service Lifecycle: Describes the release phases: Private Preview, Public Preview, and General Availability (GA) : which help teams plan adoption and manage risk.📚 7. References & Resources

item.title: Elasticsearch Explained with Real-Life Example: Why It Beats SQL for Search
item.author: Emirhan Baran
item.content:encoded: You’ve probably heard a lot about Elasticsearch recently — but what exactly is it, why should we use it, and where does it fit in? If you wonder answers of these questions you are on the right address :)Think about an e-commerce marketplace application with more than 10 million customers and more than 250,000 sellers. Billions of product listing and search queries coming every day and we have our application that is saving comments that are coming to products of the sellers in PostgreSQL DB which is an RDBMS.Over time, our sellers come with a request like this: I want to see sentiment analysis of all comments on my product lines — identify positive, neutral, negative feedback — and spot standing issues or frequently recurring complaints.If we try to use PostgreSQL for this sentiment analysis we would be have performance issues in our queries so it’s perfect time to use ElasticSearch for that because you will do full text search for the comments that are coming to products. But what exactly makes ElasticSearch different from classic RDBMS system? The answer is full-text search & inverted index.Let’s think that we are examining a comment for a headhone. There are several review for this product.If we were using PostgreSQL, we would have :SELECT * FROM reviews WHERE product= 'Headphones' and comment ILIKE '%great sound%';But the problem is searching for keywords(in our example keywords can be terrible, amazing, good, poor quality…) is very hard in RDBMS. On , this query could take several seconds or even minutes, because PostgreSQL needs to scan every row (a full table scan) to find matches.Now let’s consider we were indexing these data in ElasticSearch. Each review becomes a :{ "Review_ID": 1, "Comment": "These headphones are amazing! Great sound."{ "Product": "Headphones", "Comment": "The sound is okay, but the build feels cheap."}, "Review_ID": 3, "Comment": "Terrible quality. Do not buy."Now let’s understand how ElasticSearch handles thisElasticsearch the comment into an :amazing -> [1]great -> [1]okay -> [2]terrible -> [3]buy -> [3]Now, query:Elasticsearch looks up great → [1], and sound → [1,2].Combines results → Brings Document 1 and Document 2 with the sort of .: On the same dataset (10 million reviews), Elasticsearch typically returns results in because it uses an for O(1) term lookups, rather than scanning all text.An inverted index in Elasticsearch works like the index at the back of a book. It lets you look up a word and instantly find all the pages where it appears. Instead of scanning every row like a traditional SQL database does with a LIKE query (which can be painfully slow on billions of records), Elasticsearch breaks down each document into individual tokens (words) and stores them in this inverted index. For example, if a review says “These headphones are amazing! Great sound,” Elasticsearch will index it as amazing → [1], great → [1], sound → [1]. Another review with “The sound is okay, but the build feels cheap” adds sound → [1,2], cheap → [2]. So when you search for “great sound,” Elasticsearch simply looks up both terms in its index and returns documents 1 and 2 almost instantly-no full table scan, just a lightning-fast dictionary lookup. This is the key reason Elasticsearch outperforms traditional RDBMS for full-text search.Architecture of the ElasticSearchLet’s examine each component of the ElasticSearch together. → A cluster is a collection of one or more nodes (servers) that together store all the data and provide search and indexing capabilities. The cluster is identified by a and manages the distribution of data across nodes. → Each node is a single instance of Elasticsearch running on a server. Every node can serve as: Handles incoming client requests, routes them to the appropriate nodes (data or master), and aggregates the results before returning them to the client. It does not store data or manage the cluster state. Think of it as the “traffic controller” of Elasticsearch.— Manages the cluster state, creates or deletes indices, tracks which nodes are part of the cluster, and allocates shards. It does not handle search or indexing operations.— Stores the actual data and executes search and indexing operations. Most heavy lifting happens here.→ Data in Elasticsearch is stored in indices (similar to a database in RDBMS). Each index is split into shards for scalability. — Original data partitions. — Copies of primary shards for redundancy and high availability.Advantages of the ElasticSearch → Handles huge amounts of data by horizontally scaling (adding more nodes). Automatic sharding and replicationHigh Availability and Fault Tolerance → Replicas ensure data redundancy. If a node fails, another node with replica shards can take over.Built on Lucene, supports advanced text search features (analyzers, tokenization, relevance scoring). → JSON-Based Stores and retrieves JSON documents. Flexible schema allows dynamic mapping.→ Easy to integrate with various applications using HTTP and JSON.Open Source with Rich Ecosystem → Integrates well with Logstash and Kibana (ELK stack for log analysis and visualization).Real-Life Use Cases of Elasticsearch — E-Commerce Search & Product Discovery — Log & Event Data Analysis (ELK Stack) — Sentiment Analysis & Customer Feedback — Autocomplete & Suggestion Systems — Geospatial Search — Fraud Detection & Security AnalyticsElasticsearch doesn’t replace your relational database — it complements it. If your app needs lightning-fast text search, autocomplete, analytics, or large-scale log analysis, Elasticsearch is a must-have in your tech stack💡t: If you’re interested in setting up Elasticsearch with Spring Boot, check out my next article which we explore the integration of Spring Boot with a multi-node Elasticsearch cluster and Kibana. Also it includes practical examples of Elasticsearch queries to help you get started.

item.title: Canary Deployment and Traffic Shifting with Kubernetes and Istio
item.author: Sefik Can Kanber
item.content:encoded: Hi guys!In this article, I want to explain traffic shifting with Kubernetes and Istio, and how we can use it as part of a canary deployment strategy. Recently, I’ve been working on replatforming projects, and this technique has been a game-changer in terms of safe rollouts.🐞 Why Canary Deployment and Traffic Shifting?When deploying new versions of an application, the risk of bugs, downtime, or degraded performance is always present. Instead of pushing 100% of traffic to a new version(or new project) instantly, we use and to gradually and safely release updates.In this article, we’ll explore how to achieve traffic shifting in Kubernetes using , and walk through a complete step-by-step example.What Is a Canary Deployment?Inspired by canaries used in coal mines to detect toxic gases, are about testing new application versions on a small percentage of users. If no issues arise, the percentage is increased. If a problem occurs, you can quickly roll back without widespread impact.🔀 What Is Traffic Shifting? is the practice of controlling how much traffic is routed to different versions of a service (or even to different applications).90% of traffic → version v1 (stable app)10% of traffic → version v2 (new version or replatformed app)This approach allows real users to test the new version in production without affecting most of your traffic.🐤 What Is a Canary Deployment?Inspired by the use of canaries in coal mines to detect toxic gases, involve rolling out a new version to a small subset of users first. If no issues arise, the percentage of traffic can be increased. If something goes wrong, it’s easy to roll back with minimal impact.Kubernetes alone doesn’t offer native traffic shifting features. That’s where , a popular , comes in. It enables:Fine-grained traffic routingVersion-aware deploymentsObservability through Prometheus, GrafanaFeatures like retries, timeouts, circuit breaking, and more🛠 Sample Demo: Canary Deployment with IstioWe’ll deploy two versions of a simple application:: the current stable version: the new replatforming versionWe’ll configure Istio to send: to the stable application to the new versiontraffic-shifting/├── deployment-app1.yaml├── service.yaml├── destination-rule.yaml├── virtual-service.yamlapiVersion: apps/v1kind: Deployment name: my-app-v1 replicas: 1 matchLabels: version: v1 metadata: app: my-app spec: - name: my-app image: istio/examples-helloworld-v1 ports:apiVersion: apps/v1kind: Deployment name: my-app-v2 replicas: 1 matchLabels: version: v2 metadata: app: my-app spec: - name: my-app image: istio/examples-helloworld-v2 ports:apiVersion: v1kind: Service name: my-app selector: ports: targetPort: 5000apiVersion: networking.istio.io/v1beta1kind: DestinationRule name: my-app host: my-app - name: v1 version: v1 labels:apiVersion: networking.istio.io/v1beta1kind: VirtualService name: my-app hosts: http: - destination: subset: v1 - destination: subset: v2kubectl apply -f deployment-app1.yamlkubectl apply -f deployment-app2.yamlkubectl apply -f service.yamlkubectl apply -f destination-rule.yamlkubectl apply -f virtual-service.yaml🧪 Observing Traffic Distributionfor production-grade observability and deeper insights, it’s recommended to integrate tools like , These tools provide real-time metrics and visualizations to monitor:Request volume per versionResponse times and error ratesTraffic distribution over timeSuccess/failure ratios per service version📈 Using Prometheus & GrafanaWith Prometheus scraping Istio or Kubernetes metrics and Grafana dashboards set up, you can:Create a pie chart or time-series graph to track how traffic is being split.Alert if one version is receiving unexpected traffic share.These monitoring solutions help you validate that your traffic shifting policies are working as intended, and are essential for maintaining reliability during gradual rollouts or A/B tests.Traffic shifting allows for safer, more controlled rollouts — especially critical in production environments. With Istio and Kubernetes, implementing becomes easy, declarative, and observable.If you care about , zero-downtime deployments, and , this should definitely be part of your DevOps toolkit.

item.title: SQL Joins Explained — INNER, LEFT, RIGHT, FULL
item.author: Dbschema Pro
item.content:encoded: SQL Joins Explained — INNER, LEFT, RIGHT, FULLWhen working with databases, your data is often stored in more than one table. But how can you bring the data together?This is what SQL JOINs are for. A JOIN helps you combine rows from two tables based on a related column, usually a column that appears in both.Here are the most common types of JOINs and what they do:| JOIN Type | What it returns ||-------------|---------------------------------------------------------------------|| INNER JOIN | Only rows that exist in both tables || LEFT JOIN | All rows from the left table and matching rows from the right table || RIGHT JOIN | All rows from the right table and matching rows from the left table || FULL JOIN | All rows from both tables |One table with a list of Another table with a list of Each employee works in a department. If we want to know , we need to connect the tables using a JOIN.The JOIN compares values in both tables and brings the matching rows together.| employee_id | name | department_id ||-------------|--------------|----------------|| 1 | Sarah James | 10 || 2 | Mark White | 20 || 3 | Olivia Reed | NULL || department_id | department_name ||---------------|------------------|| 10 | Engineering || 20 | Sales || 30 | HR |SELECT name, department_nameFROM EmployeesON Employees.department_id = Departments.department_id;| name | department_name ||--------------|-----------------|| Sarah James | Engineering || Mark White | Sales |Returns only employees who are assigned to a department.Employees without a department, and departments with no employees, are not shown.Returns all employees, even if they don’t belong to any department.SELECT name, department_nameLEFT JOIN DepartmentsON Employees.department_id = Departments.department_id;| name | department_name ||--------------|-----------------|| Sarah James | Engineering || Mark White | Sales || Olivia Reed | NULL |You get . If they have no department, the result will show NULL.Returns , even if no employee is assigned to them.SELECT name, department_nameFROM EmployeesON Employees.department_id = Departments.department_id;| name | department_name ||--------------|-----------------|| Sarah James | Engineering || Mark White | Sales || NULL | HR |Returns , even if no employee is assigned to them.You get . If no one works there, the result shows NULL for the name.Returns and . If they match, they are shown together. If not, one side will be NULL.SELECT name, department_nameFROM EmployeesON Employees.department_id = Departments.department_id;| name | department_name ||--------------|-----------------|| Sarah James | Engineering || Mark White | Sales || Olivia Reed | NULL || NULL | HR |This gives you the . when you want only matching data when you want all rows from the first table when you want all rows from the second table when you want everything, matched or notYou can use DbSchema to build JOINs without writing any SQL.Drag the two tables into the canvasConnect them by dragging from one column to the other, to create a foreign key4. Right-click the line to select the 5. Choose the columns you want to display6. Run the query and see the resultsThis is a great way to , because DbSchema auto-generates SQL code for you! The JOIN types shown in the menu depend on the database you are connected to. Some databases (like SQLite) do not support FULL JOIN. In these cases, DbSchema will only show the join types supported by your database.

item.title: kftui — A better way to manage kubectl port-forwards
item.author: Henrique Cavarsan
item.content:encoded: kftui — A better way to manage kubectl port-forwardsFirst of all — kftray and kftui are independent, cross-platform applications. They help you set up and manage multiple port-forwarding settings easily. Both apps are basically the same tool with different interfaces. kftray is the GUI version with windows and buttons. kftui is the terminal version that runs in your command line. They share the same database and configs. Set up a port-forward in kftray’s GUI, and you’ll see it in kftui too. Save something in kftui, and it shows up in kftray. You can use whichever one you prefer or switch between them.So… the kftray/kftui docs are pretty messy right now, and I figured writing this blog post would be easier than fixing everything properly. I’ll organize the docs later, but this should cover what kftui does and how to use it.kftui has most of the same stuff as kftray, but this guide is just about the terminal version.Why Build a Terminal Interface?I built kftui to address some practical limitations of GUI applications. Terminal interfaces use significantly fewer system resources, which matters in resource-constrained environments. They work well over SSH connections without requiring X11 forwarding or remote desktop setups.From a development perspective, maintaining one codebase in Rust is simpler than managing separate frontend and backend projects. This approach reduces complexity and keeps the tool focused on core functionality.If you’ve worked with multiple kubectl port-forward processes in different terminal windows, you know how quickly it becomes difficult to manage. kftui provides a centralized way to handle these connections.You’ll need curl or wget installed. Windows users need PowerShell.Unix-like systems (Linux, macOS, WSL):brew tap hcavarsan/kftraybrew install kftuibash <(curl -s https://raw.githubusercontent.com/hcavarsan/kftray/main/hacks/kftui_installer.sh)bash <(wget -qO- https://raw.githubusercontent.com/hcavarsan/kftray/main/hacks/kftui_installer.sh)Invoke-Expression ((New-Object System.Net.WebClient).DownloadString('https://raw.githubusercontent.com/hcavarsan/kftray/main/hacks/kftui_installer.ps1'))After installation, restart your terminal and verify it works:Start with a simple configuration file. Create config.json:[ { "context": "minikube", "service": "api-service", "remote_port": 80, "workload_type": "service"]Launch kftui with this configuration:kftui --configs-path config.jsonThe interface displays your configuration in the “Stopped” table on the left. Press f to start the port-forward. The configuration moves to the "Running" table on the right, and your service becomes accessible at localhost:8080. Press f again to stop it.To avoid specifying the config file path repeatedly, save configurations to kftui’s database:kftui --configs-path config.json --saveAfter saving, run kftui without arguments to access your stored configurations. This approach works well for frequently used port-forward setups.kftui supports several ways to load configurations, each suited to different workflows.Load configurations from local JSON files:kftui --configs-path /path/to/config.jsonImport configurations from version-controlled repositories:kftui --github-url https://github.com/your-team/k8s-configs --configs-path environments/dev.jsonThis method works well for teams that maintain environment-specific configurations in version control.Pass JSON configuration directly for scripting scenarios:kftui --json '[{"alias":"test","namespace":"default","service":"my-service","local_port":8080,"remote_port":80,"protocol":"tcp","workload_type":"service"}]'Read configurations from stdin for pipeline integration:echo '[{"alias":"api",...}]' | kftui --stdin: Store configurations in the database for future use: Clear existing configurations before importing new ones: Start all port-forwards immediately after loading: Run without the interface for automation scriptsRather than manually creating configuration files, kftui can discover services through Kubernetes annotations. This approach keeps port-forward configurations synchronized with service definitions.Add annotations to your Kubernetes services:apiVersion: v1kind: Service name: monitoring-stack annotations: kftray.app/configs: "grafana-3000-3000,prometheus-9090-9090,alertmanager-9093-9093"spec: app: monitoring - name: grafana - name: prometheus - name: alertmanagerThe annotation format is alias-local_port-remote_port. Multiple configurations are separated by commas.To use auto-discovery, navigate to the top menu in kftui and select “Auto Add”. Choose your Kubernetes context from the available options, and kftui will create configurations for all annotated services in that context.The interface consists of four main areas:: Contains Help, Auto Add, Import, Export, Settings, About, and Exit options (left): Displays configurations ready to start (right): Shows active port-forwards with status information (bottom): Provides information about the selected configurationThe top menu provides seven functions:: Display usage information and keyboard shortcuts: Discover services from your Kubernetes cluster: Load configuration files through a file browser: Save current configurations to a JSON file: Configure application behavior: Show version and project information: Stop all active port-forwards and close the applicationNavigate menu items with ←/→ arrow keys and press Enter to select.Access settings by pressing s or selecting Settings from the menu.: Configure automatic disconnection after a specified period of inactivity. Set to 0 to disable automatic timeouts.: Enable or disable network connectivity monitoring. When enabled, kftui monitors network status and attempts to reconnect dropped port-forwards.Settings persist between application sessions.kftui provides HTTP request and response logging for debugging network traffic through port-forwards.Enable logging by setting "http_logs_enabled": true in your configuration file, or toggle it for existing configurations by pressing l in the interface.Press Shift+V to open the HTTP logs viewer, which operates in two modes:List Mode (default view):Displays all HTTP requests with timestamps and status codesNavigate requests with ↑/↓ arrow keysUse PageUp/PageDown to scroll through multiple entriesPress Enter on any request to view detailed informationPress a to toggle automatic scrolling for new requestsDetail Mode (activated by pressing Enter on a request):Shows complete request and response information including headers and body contentScroll through details with ↑/↓ arrow keysUse PageUp/PageDown for faster navigationPress r to replay the selected requestPress Esc to return to list modePress L to configure HTTP logging behavior:Enable or disable logging for individual configurationsSet maximum log file size (1–1000 MB)Configure retention period (1–365 days)Enable automatic cleanup of old log filesComplete Keyboard ReferenceKey Function f Start or stop port-forward i Import configurations e Export configurations d Delete selected configurations a Select all configurations Space Toggle individual selection Tab Switch between interface components ←/→ Navigate menu items s Open settings h Show help information q Show about information l Toggle HTTP logging L Configure HTTP logging V View HTTP logs o Open HTTP logs in external editor ↑/↓ Navigate within sections PageUp/PageDown Scroll through content Home/End Jump to first/last item Ctrl+C Exit applicationConfiguration File StructureConfiguration files use JSON format with the following fields:{ "alias": "my-service", // Display name in interface "namespace": "production", // Kubernetes namespace (required) "service": "api-service", // Target service name "local_port": 8080, // Local binding port "remote_port": 80, // Remote service port "protocol": "tcp", // Network protocol (required) "workload_type": "service", // Target type: "service" or "pod" "context": "prod-cluster", // Kubernetes context "kubeconfig": "/path/to/config", // Kubeconfig file path "http_logs_enabled": true // Enable HTTP logging}The minimum required fields are namespace and protocol. When ports are not specified, kftui attempts to determine appropriate values from the service definition.kftui handles pretty much everything the kftray GUI does, just through terminal commands and keyboard shortcuts. The auto-discovery stuff saves time by reading your Kubernetes service annotations instead of making you type configs manually. The HTTP logging is useful for debugging API calls and seeing what requests are going through your port-forwards.If you work mostly in terminals or need to manage port-forwards over SSH, kftui covers everything without needing a desktop environment.In this quick demo video, I’ve tested both tools with the same setup — port forwarding to a service while running curl in a loop. Then deleted all pods with kubectl delete pods — all — force to see how each handles recovery.kubectl port forward: When pods get deleted, the port forward just dies even though it’s forwarding to a service. All requests fail and you have to manually restart it.kftray: Loses maybe one request when pods get deleted. The watcher detects changes immediately and reconnects to new pods as they come up. The curl loop keeps going like nothing happened.If you find kftray or kftui useful, consider starring the project on GitHub ⭐
